citation.sourceDocument: Wisconsin DOR 2026 rate schedule (Wis. Stat. §71.06) + §71.05(23) sliding standard deduction + $1,200 personal exemption (2025 Wisconsin Act 15)
citation.sourceNote: Wisconsin's four rates for tax year 2026 — 3.50% / 4.40% / 5.30% / 7.65% (Wis. Stat. §71.06) — apply over inflation-indexed thresholds that differ by filing status: single 4.40% at $15,110, 5.30% at $51,950, 7.65% at $332,720; married filing jointly 4.40% at $20,150, 5.30% at $69,260, 7.65% at $443,630. The elevated 5.30% threshold ($51,950 single, up from a purely-indexed ~$29,000) reflects the 2025-27 biennial budget (2025 Wisconsin Act 15, signed 2025-07-03), which widened the second (4.40%) bracket so more income is taxed at the lower rate. Wisconsin's signature feature is its SLIDING standard deduction (§71.05(23)(a)): a maximum of $13,960 (single) / $25,840 (married jointly) that is reduced by a flat percentage of Wisconsin AGI above a threshold — 12% over $20,119 for single (reaching $0 at $136,453), 19.778% over $29,039 for joint (reaching $0 at $159,690) — modeled via the engine's standardDeductionPhaseOut `reductionRate` form (the Wisconsin variant the roadmap had deferred alongside South Carolina's divisor form). On top of the deduction, Wisconsin allows a $1,200-per-exemption personal exemption — raised from $700 by 2025 Act 15, effective tax year 2025 — subtracted from income ($1,200 single / head of household, $2,400 married jointly). Head of household is mapped to the single schedule and deduction at launch fidelity: Wisconsin's true HoH standard deduction starts higher ($9,300 base) and phases out in two segments (22.515% until it converges with the single curve, then 12%), and HoH brackets sit just wider than single, so modeling HoH as single understates the deduction and the bracket widths — the figure errs slightly high, the conservative side. The $1,200-per-dependent exemption, the $250 age-65 exemptions, the married-couple credit, the school-property-tax and other Wisconsin credits, and the WAGI-rounded lookup tables (the engine uses the continuous statutory formula) are omitted at launch fidelity. Wisconsin levies no local income tax. Worked example, single $60,000 AGI: deduction = $13,960 − 12%·(60,000 − 20,119) = $13,960 − $4,785.72 = $9,174.28; taxable = 60,000 − 9,174.28 − 1,200 = $49,625.72; tax = 3.50%·15,110 + 4.40%·(49,625.72 − 15,110) = $528.85 + $1,518.69 = $2,047.54. The brackets and standard deduction index annually; the refresh adapter anchors the standard-deduction maximum and the per-status bracket tables roll alongside it as the reviewer's data-only step. Verified against the Tax Foundation 2026 state-rate table, the Wisconsin DOR individual income tax page, Wis. Stat. §§71.05(23)/71.06, and 2025 Wisconsin Act 15 on 2026-06-06.
